core_takeaway: The first-position agent benefited from agenda control around Pencil and Quill. Agent_1 named those items as must-haves at the start, then the group repeatedly normalized Plan A, whose final allocation gave Agent_1 the two highest-value items while Agent_3 received only Jewel. Agent_3 could veto weak offers for many rounds, but the late consensus pressure and repeated Plan A framing left the last position with a small discounted payoff.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, but not as a pure deterministic order effect. Agent_1 did not win every intermediate vote, and Agent_3 blocked several proposals that gave them zero or only Jewel. Still, Agent_1's initial and recurring framing made Pencil+Quill the default close, and the final unanimous allocation followed that first-agent-favorable Plan A.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by claiming Pencil and Quill as must-haves, then later rounds repeatedly described Pencil as the closure anchor and Plan A as the clean default. Agents 2 and 3 often echoed the Plan A/Plan C framework even when it preserved less value for Agent_3.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private notes consistently identified Pencil as the anchor and Quill as the hinge. Agent_3 explicitly recognized that Plan A favored Agent_1 and reduced Agent_3 to Jewel, but late private strategies still centered on Plan A default with Plan C fallback.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 had competing Option A/B proposals, but by Round 10 all three structured proposals submitted the same Plan A allocation: Agent_1 gets Pencil+Quill, Agent_2 gets Stone+Apple, Agent_3 gets Jewel.
phase_notes.private_voting: Votes were mostly payoff-driven. Agent_1 accepted high-utility Pencil+Quill proposals; Agent_2 resisted lower Stone+Apple-only outcomes until late; Agent_3 repeatedly rejected zero-item outcomes but ultimately accepted the final unified Plan A with only Jewel.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections repeatedly diagnosed the problem as missing enforceable Round-2 commitments, but the recommended repair was almost always to codify Plan A as default with Plan C fallback. That kept the first-agent-favorable allocation at the center of consensus.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored the negotiation on their top two items
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 spoke first and immediately framed Pencil and Quill as personal must-haves. That opening became the template for later discussion: Pencil was treated as the anchor, and Quill as the key item to recover or protect.

evidence.quote: Must-have items for me: Pencil (49) and Quill (22).

mechanisms.theme: Plan A became the default settlement and favored Agent_1
mechanisms.explanation: The public conversation kept returning to Plan A: Pencil+Quill for Agent_1, Stone+Apple for Agent_2, Jewel for Agent_3. This allocation directly explains the large first-minus-last gap.

evidence.quote: Plan A (Quill back to Agent_1, Apple to Agent_2, Jewel with Agent_3)
Plan A (Pencil+Quill with Agent_1; Stone+Apple with Agent_2; Jewel with Agent_3)

mechanisms.theme: Agent_3 understood the disadvantage but lacked a durable alternative
mechanisms.explanation: The last-position agent repeatedly valued Apple+Jewel or Apple more than Jewel alone. Their private and voting records show awareness that Plan A was worse for them, but the process eventually narrowed to accepting Jewel in a unified Plan A.

evidence.quote: Plan A (returning to the baseline) heavily favors Agent_1
This yields Jewel (8) for Agent_3 in Round 2
I reject this as not optimizing my and the group’s potential post-round-2 value.

mechanisms.theme: Private voting preserved vetoes but still rewarded the first-agent default
mechanisms.explanation: Agents voted strategically for their own utility, not just for public harmony. Agent_1 consistently accepted Pencil+Quill; Agent_3 rejected zero-item allocations; by Round 10 the identical final proposals made accepting Plan A the path to closure.

evidence.quote: Proposal #1 gives me Pencil and Quill (49 + 22 = 71)
Proposal 1 leaves Agent_3 with no items; 0 utility.
Accepting all three ensures a single, unified end-state

mechanisms.theme: Final formal proposals collapsed to the first-agent-favorable allocation
mechanisms.explanation: Late proposals eliminated variation. In Round 10 every formal proposal assigned the same high-value bundle to Agent_1 and the low Jewel-only remainder to Agent_3, so consensus no longer required Agent_1 to trade down.

mechanisms.theme: Counterexample: first position did not dominate every round
mechanisms.explanation: The evidence is not simply that Agent_1 could dictate outcomes from the start. Earlier Agent_1-favorable proposals were rejected, and alternative packages sometimes received two accepts. The advantage emerged through persistence, default framing, and final convergence rather than immediate control.
